feedback.text: It took a lot of lawmaking to facilitate fairness in products and services. This was done so both could be kept within the realm of consumer safety while also ever modernizing. Well, that was in the real world. On the internet, the entire challenge started anew. And it's so much harder because the web is ultimately comprised of software. Is software a service? Is software a product? It appears to be both. Or at least classification would have to be made by observing the type of software, what its function is, how it is provided, and so forth. My point is: There remains a great deal of thinking - and lawmaking - to be done on the complexity as well as apparent unity of products and services within the digital space. And the validity of entitlements within it. Entitlements such as ownership and access rights. Today, countless online consumers spend immense amounts of money on software. They do this under the well-meaning assumption that they are making one-time payments for permanent access and use of said software. A safe and legally protected lifetime license if you will. This assumption however is very far from the truth. Software distributors may suggest to you that your purchase with them is a genuine act of buying, that you're acquiring a tangible product and the right to use it forever, but their terms of service ubiquitously paint the opposed picture - everything is rendered a mere service; obligations by the distributor are minimal; even a software copy that could function autonomously indefinitely can legally be rendered inoperable by the distributor via remote online access. Provision of an alternative download source in case of unforeseen events? No legal requirement whatsoever for such a thing. And if consumers even attempt to edit source code in order to keep purchased software usable despite publisher plans to the contrary, they risk making themselves guilty of copyright violation and severe legal ramifications. As a software consumer you give a lot and receive shockingly little. You see, as someone who has spent many thousands of Euros on software and will continue to spend many thousand Euros more, it concerns me to great lengths that my access to those purchases is at the absolute mercy of the companies I made them with. Should the consumer not have more pull in this relationship? I'm not sure what rights I could name if, for example, US-based video game distributor Valve Corporation decided to cite new terms of service under which they can no longer provide any services to me. Please protect the ownership and licensing rights of software consumers. The European Union is the only provider of help and betterment that we can hope for.
